items.q: Why does reactivity increase down Group 2?
items.correct: Atomic radius increases and ionisation energies decrease, so the two outer electrons are lost more easily.
items.misconception: Reactivity increases because the atoms have more electrons to give away.
items.random_wrong: Reactivity increases because the nuclear charge decreases down the group.
items.why: Students count total electrons instead of thinking about how easily the two outer electrons are removed; every Group 2 metal loses exactly two.

items.q: Why is chlorine a stronger oxidising agent than iodine?
items.correct: Chlorine atoms are smaller, so the incoming electron is closer to the nucleus, less shielded, and gained more readily.
items.misconception: Iodine is the stronger oxidising agent because it is bigger and has more electrons.
items.random_wrong: Chlorine is stronger only because it is a gas at room temperature.
items.why: Students transfer the Group 1 'more reactive down the group' trend to halogens, where the trend reverses because halogens gain rather than lose electrons.

items.q: Can silver nitrate solution distinguish between halide ions?
items.correct: Yes — AgCl is white, AgBr cream and AgI yellow, and their different solubilities in ammonia confirm the identification.
items.misconception: All silver halide precipitates look identical, so silver nitrate cannot tell halides apart.
items.random_wrong: Silver nitrate turns each halide solution a different shade of blue.
items.why: Students remember 'a precipitate forms' but not that the colour and ammonia solubility differ, which is the whole point of the test.

items.q: Which Group 2 hydroxide is least soluble in water?
items.why: Correct answer: Mg(OH)₂. “Ca(OH)₂” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which halogen will displace bromide ions from aqueous solution?
items.why: Correct answer: Chlorine. “Iodine” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: When chlorine reacts with cold dilute NaOH, the chlorine is:
items.why: It's Simultaneously oxidised and reduced. “Only oxidised” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which halide ion is the strongest reducing agent?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: I⁻. “F⁻” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the oxidation state of chlorine in NaClO (sodium chlorate(I))?
items.why: Exact answer: 1. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
